questions.question: Explain the problem of null pointers in binary trees and how threaded binary trees solve it.
keyPoints: Binary tree with n nodes has 2n pointers but only n-1 are used for edges
This results in n+1 null pointers being wasted
Threaded binary trees repurpose these null pointers to store traversal threads
Threads point to inorder predecessor or successor instead of being NULL
This enables O(1) space traversal without stack or recursion

questions.question: Define the node structure for a double-threaded binary tree and explain the purpose of each field.
keyPoints: Data field: stores the actual node value
Left pointer: points to left child or inorder predecessor
Right pointer: points to right child or inorder successor
Ltag: 0 indicates thread (predecessor), 1 indicates child
Rtag: 0 indicates thread (successor), 1 indicates child
Total of 5 fields per node

questions.question: Describe the algorithm for inorder traversal in a right-threaded binary tree.
keyPoints: Start from root and go to leftmost node (while ltag=1)
Visit the node
If rtag=0 (thread), move to inorder successor using right pointer
If rtag=1 (actual child), move to right child and find its leftmost descendant
Repeat until NULL is reached

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of using threaded binary trees?
keyPoints: Advantages: O(1) space for traversal, no recursion/stack needed, efficient for static trees, fast inorder traversal
Disadvantages: Complex insertion and deletion operations, cannot easily support all tree operations, additional tag fields increase node size slightly

questions.question: Differentiate between single-threaded and double-threaded binary trees.
keyPoints: Single-threaded: uses only one null pointer direction (either left or right) for threading
Double-threaded: uses both left and right null pointers for threading
Single-threaded allows traversal in one direction only
Double-threaded allows bidirectional traversal (to predecessor and successor)
Double-threaded requires two tag fields, single-threaded requires one

questions.question: Explain Morris Inorder Traversal and how it achieves O(1) space complexity.
keyPoints: Morris traversal temporarily creates threads to find inorder predecessor
Uses the null right pointers to create temporary links back to predecessor
After processing each node, the tree structure is restored
No recursion or explicit stack is used
Achieves O(n) time with O(1) auxiliary space

questions.question: How many null pointers exist in a binary tree with 100 nodes? Prove this statement.
keyPoints: A binary tree with n nodes has 2n total pointers
n-1 pointers are used to create n-1 edges connecting nodes
Therefore, null pointers = 2n - (n-1) = n+1
For n=100: null pointers = 100+1 = 101
This is the fundamental property exploited by threaded binary trees

questions.question: Explain the concept of threading in binary trees. Discuss the types of threaded binary trees with suitable diagrams. Also write the algorithm for creating a right-inorder threaded binary tree.
keyPoints: Threading concept: using null pointers to store predecessor/successor information
Types: Single (right-inorder, left-inorder), Double (both directions)
Right-inorder threading: fill null right pointers with inorder successors

Algorithm: Initialize previous as NULL, perform inorder traversal, for each node if left is NULL set left thread to previous, if previous's right is NULL set right thread to current, update previous to current
Time complexity O(n), space O(1) for traversal during creation

questions.question: Write a program to implement inorder traversal of a threaded binary tree. Explain the algorithm with proper comments and analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Find leftmost node, then iteratively visit nodes following threads
While current is not NULL: move to leftmost (ltag=1), visit node, follow right threads (rtag=0) or move to right child
Time complexity: O(n) - visits each node once
Space complexity: O(1) - no stack or recursion used
Compare with traditional traversal requiring O(h) or O(n) space

questions.question: Compare threaded binary trees with Morris traversal technique. Discuss the scenarios where each approach is preferred.
keyPoints: Threaded trees: permanent modification, threads stored permanently, good for frequently traversed static trees
Morris traversal: temporary modification, threads created and removed during traversal, good for one-time traversal
Threaded trees have overhead for insertion/deletion
Morris is better when tree modification is not desired and traversal is occasional
Both achieve O(1) auxiliary space for traversal
Morris is simpler to implement as it doesn't require pre-processing

questions.question: Design the node structure for a binary tree that supports both left and right threading. Write an algorithm to insert a new node in such a threaded binary tree and explain the challenges involved.
keyPoints: Node structure: data, left, right, ltag, rtag (5 fields)
Insertion is complex because threads must be updated
Challenges: Find correct position using threading, update predecessor's thread, update successor's thread, set tags correctly for new node and affected nodes
Unlike regular BST insertion, every thread pointer that should point through the new node must be updated
This complexity makes threaded trees unsuitable for frequently modified trees
